model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget on a flat MLP, the baseline only processes about 16k examples. Increasing batch size to 256 lets training cover the full 50k-example set within the checker budget, which is often a larger gain than small architecture tweaks here. Raising Adam's learning rate to 1e-3 matches the larger batch and should improve optimization progress without adding complexity.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current schedule and batch setup are working, so the next low-risk lever is optimizer dynamics. Switching from Adam to AdamW keeps the same fast adaptive behavior while decoupling weight decay, which often improves short-budget MLP training; slightly reducing decay should preserve regularization without over-constraining a model that only sees about one pass over the data.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The current AdamW plus warmup-cosine setup is already strong, so the next efficient probe is LR scale. With batch size 256 and only 256 training steps, a somewhat higher peak rate can improve useful progress within the short budget without adding complexity or changing the training contract.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization setup is now reasonably tuned for the short 256-step budget, so the next promising lever is modestly increasing MLP capacity. Raising the base width from 192/384 hidden units to 256/512 should improve representation quality with a small, safe change and without adding architectural complexity like extra depth.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last width increase helped materially, which suggests the current setup is still capacity-limited more than optimization-limited. Adding one more hidden block is a simple way to increase expressiveness under the same tuned optimizer and schedule, and the fixed 256-step budget is still likely sufficient for this modest depth increase.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Both recent layout edits improved loss, so the model still appears capacity-limited under the current optimizer and schedule. Increasing base width again is the simplest continuation of that trend: it strengthens all hidden layers while keeping the same training loop and avoiding more invasive changes.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The model has become larger and deeper while recent capacity gains are still positive, so retuning LR scale is the next cheap probe. A modest increase to 2e-3 should let the larger network use the short 256-step budget more aggressively without changing the rest of the training setup.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last LR increase regressed validation loss, so the immediate best move is to step back to the previously better rate rather than stack more changes on a degraded setting. This restores the strongest known optimization scale for the current deeper, wider MLP and preserves a clean base for subsequent experiments.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The current model is training under a very short fixed budget, so underfitting is still a plausible limiter even after the capacity increases. Reducing AdamW weight decay is a simple way to relax regularization and let the larger MLP use more of its capacity without changing the optimizer family or schedule.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The strongest improvements have come from increasing model capacity, and the current configuration still trains stably under the fixed budget. A further width increase is the cleanest next test: it preserves the same optimization recipe while probing whether validation loss continues to improve with a somewhat larger hidden representation.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last width increase degraded validation loss, so the cleanest next step is to return to the previously best-performing capacity setting. Restoring the smaller width recovers the strongest known architecture for the current optimizer and schedule before testing a different axis.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 320","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The current configuration is stable and appears to benefit from better use of the fixed 256-step budget. Increasing batch size modestly to 320 is a targeted way to expose the model to more training examples without the larger runtime jump of moving all the way to 384 or 512, making it a good late-stage probe.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 320","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last batch-size increase produced the largest recent gain, which strongly suggests the model still benefits from seeing more examples within the fixed 256-step budget. Moving from 320 to 384 is the most direct follow-up: it continues that trend with a moderate runtime increase and without changing the now-strong optimizer or architecture setup.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Successive batch-size increases have been the most reliable source of improvement, indicating that this workload benefits heavily from seeing more examples within the fixed 256-step budget. Increasing to 512 is the strongest remaining simple lever on that axis and is worth testing while there are still a few steps left.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 640","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch size has been the strongest and most consistent improvement axis by a wide margin, suggesting this fixed-step workload is dominated by examples-seen rather than update count alone. Increasing from 512 to 640 is the highest-signal next experiment because it extends the winning trend while being a more conservative step than jumping straight to very large batches.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 640","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch size continues to be the dominant improvement axis, with repeated gains as more examples are processed within the fixed 256-step budget. Increasing from 640 to 768 is the highest-signal next move because it extends the strongest trend while remaining a moderate step rather than an aggressive jump.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch size has dominated every other intervention, with repeated improvements even at large values, so the final step should push the strongest lever. Moving to 1024 maximizes examples seen per fixed update budget and is the highest-upside final experiment consistent with the observed trend.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}
